masthead.dek: Forty-one questions across the world's pantheons — Greek, Norse, Egyptian, and beyond. Every one rides a slicer or a reflex-defeater (Odin lost an eye, but who lost a hand?), so you reason from the story rather than recall a name. Narrow it down, place your bet, then tap to check.
masthead.footer_html: Forty-one questions spanning Greek, Roman, Norse, Egyptian, Hindu, Japanese, Aztec, Mesopotamian, and Celtic myth — verified to Theoi, Britannica, and World History Encyclopedia.

dimensions: a god who became a mother
the holdout who doomed a rescue
villain who was once a defender
ruler vs embalmer of the dead
trick that beat the sun god
first to die rules the dead
how a god got an animal head
the sun hides in a cave
plan.note: Built on slicers and reflex-defeaters across pantheons; cross-mythology questions fix the culture to defeat the obvious (e.g. Orpheus) answer. Answer set rotates the Olympians, heroes, monsters, and the named gods of each tradition.

questions.q: Three brothers split the world by lot — sky, sea, and the dead. The one who drew the dead is a son of Cronus and brother of Zeus, yet he's usually left off the list of the twelve Olympians. Name him.
questions.detail: Defined by residence on Olympus, the twelve exclude Hades because his realm is the underworld.
questions.why: 'Son of Cronus + brother of Zeus' screams core Olympian; 'left off the twelve' points to the one whose realm is elsewhere.
questions.more: Hestia is sometimes dropped too, but she lives on Olympus tending the hearth.

questions.q: One Olympian is counted as the newest at the table, taking a throne a gentler goddess gave up to keep the number at twelve. Which god is this late arrival?
questions.detail: Hestia yields her throne so Dionysus can join, keeping the count at twelve.
questions.why: Players reach for an 'old' god; 'newest, took a vacated seat' isolates the wine god.
questions.more: Athens' lists disagreed — the Parthenon frieze shows Dionysus among the twelve.

questions.q: He's the only god ever cast out of Olympus who later returned to live there. Hurled from the mountain as an infant, he grew into the gods' master craftsman. Name him.
questions.detail: Thrown from Olympus, he landed on Lemnos — the only Olympian exiled then restored, coaxed back by Dionysus.
questions.why: 'Cast out yet returned' plus 'master craftsman' uniquely fingerprints the smith.
questions.more: His lameness traces to that fall in one version.

questions.q: Among the twelve, one goddess in the most famous account was born from no marriage at all — she rose from sea foam stirred when a Titan's severed flesh fell into the water. Name her.
questions.detail: In Hesiod she rises from the foam around the castrated flesh of Uranus — not the child of any divine couple.
questions.why: Reflex says 'daughter of Zeus'; 'born from no marriage, from sea foam' selects the love goddess in her oldest origin.
questions.more: Her name was popularly linked to 'aphros,' Greek for foam.

questions.q: Of all the mortal-born heroes, only one ended his story by burning away his human half and being welcomed onto Olympus as a true god, married to the cupbearer Hebe. Name him.
questions.detail: His mortal nature burned away on the pyre, his divine essence taken to Olympus, where he wed Hebe.
questions.why: Many heroes are demigods; 'became a full god, married Hebe' is the unique apotheosis fingerprint.
questions.more: Even Hera, his tormentor, was reconciled to him at his deification.

questions.q: Three snake-haired sisters could turn onlookers to stone, but two of them could never die. A hero's whole quest was possible only because the third one could. Name that mortal sister.
questions.detail: Only Medusa was mortal; Stheno and Euryale were immortal.
questions.why: 'Snake-haired, turns to stone' reflexively says Gorgon; 'the only one who could die' singles out Medusa.
questions.more: From her severed neck sprang Pegasus and the giant Chrysaor.

questions.q: A fire-breathing beast — lion in front, goat in the middle, serpent for a tail — was killed from above by a hero riding a winged horse. Name the hero, not the horse.
questions.detail: Mounted on Pegasus, he killed the Chimera by jamming a lead-tipped spear into its mouth, where the flame melted the lead.
questions.why: The winged horse pulls minds toward Pegasus or Perseus; 'name the hero' plus the Chimera force the answer.
questions.more: Pegasus had earlier sprung from Medusa.

questions.cat: Mythology · immortal who chose to die
questions.q: A wise tutor of heroes, born immortal, was struck by a poisoned arrow whose agony could not kill him. He chose to surrender his deathlessness so another could be freed. Name him.
questions.detail: The learned centaur gave up his immortality so Prometheus could be released; he was set among the stars as Centaurus.
questions.why: 'Immortals can't die' is the whole defeater — the answer is the famous exception.
questions.more: He tutored Achilles, Jason, and the healer Asclepius.

questions.q: A wrestling giant forced travelers to fight him and couldn't be beaten on the ground, since contact with his mother renewed his strength. Heracles won only by holding him in the air. Name the giant.
questions.detail: Son of Poseidon and Gaia, he drew strength from touching the Earth; Heracles lifted him aloft and crushed him.
questions.why: Players expect brute strength to win wrestling; his power came from the ground.
questions.more: His mother was Gaia, exactly why losing contact with the earth doomed him.

questions.q: A huntress sworn to virginity outran every suitor in a footrace, until one beat her by tossing golden apples to slow her down. Name her.
questions.detail: Suitors had to beat her in a race or die; she lost when a suitor dropped three golden apples for her to chase.
questions.why: The all-male hero roster makes a woman the surprising fit; 'footrace lost to golden apples' uniquely names her.
questions.more: She's also counted as the only woman among the Argonauts.

questions.q: Among the famous sinners punished in Tartarus, one is doomed to shove a boulder up a hill forever, watching it roll back each time he nears the top. Name him.
questions.detail: He must eternally roll a boulder uphill only for it to roll down — distinct from Tantalus and Ixion.
questions.why: 'Eternal punishment' cues several names; the specific task slices the trio to the boulder-roller.
questions.more: Tantalus (receding food) and Ixion (fiery wheel) share his neighborhood.

questions.q: One Olympian alone moves freely among the heavens, the earth, and the land of the dead, escorting souls to the river of the underworld. Name him.
questions.detail: Hermes Psychopompos uniquely passes between Olympus, earth, and Hades, guiding souls to Charon.
questions.why: Reflex 'underworld' pulls toward Hades or Persephone, who live there; 'moves freely among all three' selects the traveler.
questions.more: His winged sandals and herald's staff are the badges of that role.

questions.q: Powerful and feared, this Olympian was nonetheless the most hated of all the gods — even Zeus told him so to his face. A rival deity stood for the disciplined, strategic side of the same domain. Name the hated god.
questions.detail: In the Iliad Zeus calls Ares the most hateful of the gods to him; Ares is brutal war, Athena its strategic side.
questions.why: 'Father openly hated him, paired against a strategic rival in the same domain' names the war god disdained by Olympus.
questions.more: Athena repeatedly bests and mocks him in myth.

questions.q: Dipped as a baby in a magic river, this warrior was unharmable everywhere except the one spot his mother gripped. He died from a single arrow there. Name him.
questions.detail: Thetis dipped him in the Styx, leaving only the heel she held vulnerable; Paris's arrow struck it.
questions.why: Recognizable hero, but the dimension (a lone vulnerability) is unique and the answer plainly defensible.
questions.more: The phrase 'Achilles' heel' comes straight from this.

questions.q: Winter comes, the Greeks said, from a goddess's grief: her daughter must spend part of each year below because she ate a few pomegranate seeds. Name the grieving mother, not the daughter.
questions.detail: The grain goddess lets the earth go barren in mourning while Persephone is below, bound by the pomegranate seeds.
questions.why: The seeds and underworld pull toward Persephone; 'name the mother' forces the harvest goddess.
questions.more: The number of seeds is said to set how many months Persephone stays below.

questions.q: Gods are deathless, yet one ancient story reports a god's death announced across the sea to a passing sailor. This horned god of the wild is the one the tale names as dead. Name him.
questions.detail: Plutarch records the cry 'the great god Pan is dead' carried to the sailor Thamus.
questions.why: 'Gods can't die' is the defeater; the exception is the goat-legged god of wild places.
questions.more: Early Christian writers read it as a sign paganism was passing.

questions.q: A hundred-eyed watchman was set to guard a girl Zeus loved; after Hermes killed him, a goddess saved his many eyes by setting them in the tail of her sacred bird. Name the watchman.
questions.detail: The all-seeing hundred-eyed giant guarded Io; after Hermes slew him, Hera placed his eyes on the peacock's tail.
questions.why: The peacock's 'eyes' are familiar, but tracing them to a named guardian is the surprising twist.
questions.more: His epithet Panoptes means 'all-seeing.'

questions.q: To kill a monster whose direct gaze turned men to stone, this hero never looked at her — he watched her reflection in a polished shield and struck. Name the hero.
questions.detail: He beheaded Medusa by viewing only her reflection in a bronze shield from Athena.
questions.why: The monster cue pulls toward naming Medusa; the stem asks for the hero, and 'fought by reflection' is his signature.
questions.more: He also wore Hades's helmet of invisibility and Hermes's winged sandals.

questions.q: One Norse god let a monstrous wolf bite off his hand so the other gods could chain it up. Odin is famous for losing an eye — this was a different god losing a different part. Who lost the hand?
questions.detail: Tyr placed his hand in Fenrir's mouth as a pledge while the gods bound the wolf; Fenrir bit it off.
questions.why: 'Lost a body part' surfaces Odin; the wolf detail prunes to the one-handed god of law.
questions.more: Tyr's willingness made the binding 'legal' in the eyes of the myth.

questions.cat: Mythology · a god who became a mother
questions.dimension: a god who became a mother
questions.q: Odin's eight-legged horse Sleipnir had an unusual mother: a Norse god who turned into a mare and gave birth to it. Which god was the mother?
questions.detail: Loki shapeshifted into a mare to sabotage a giant builder's deal, was impregnated, and bore Sleipnir.
questions.why: Among Norse gods, only the shapeshifting trickster fits 'a god who became a mother.'
questions.more: He fathered Fenrir, Jormungandr, and Hel — but Sleipnir he bore.

questions.q: To seal the truce ending their war, the Norse gods all spat into a vat, and from that spittle they made a being so wise he could answer any question. Who was made from the gods' spit?
questions.detail: Kvasir was created from the combined saliva of the Aesir and Vanir; dwarfs later brewed his blood into the Mead of Poetry.
questions.why: 'Made from spit / answers anything' points only to Kvasir, sidestepping Odin or Mimir.
questions.more: Whoever drinks the Mead of Poetry becomes a poet or scholar.

questions.q: At Ragnarok, the wolf Fenrir swallows Odin. One of Odin's other sons then avenges him by killing Fenrir and lives on into the renewed world. Which son?
questions.detail: Vidar, the silent god, tears Fenrir's jaws apart with a special thick shoe and survives Ragnarok.
questions.why: Among Odin's sons, the avenger-who-survives is Vidar, not the more famous Thor (who dies killing Jormungandr).
questions.more: Other survivors include Thor's sons Modi and Magni, and Baldr.

questions.q: Frigg made every thing in the world swear never to harm her son Baldr, but skipped one plant she thought too young to bother. Loki used that plant to kill Baldr. What plant was it?
questions.detail: Loki fashioned the overlooked mistletoe into a dart and guided the blind god Hodr to throw it, killing Baldr.
questions.why: The murder weapon is a soft, harmless plant rather than a blade — counterintuitive and uniquely pinned.
questions.more: This oversight sets Ragnarok in motion.

questions.cat: Mythology · the holdout who doomed a rescue
questions.dimension: the holdout who doomed a rescue
questions.q: Hel agreed to release the dead Baldr if every single thing in the world wept for him. Everything wept except one giantess who refused, dooming him. By what name did this holdout go?
questions.detail: The giantess Thokk refused to weep, widely believed to be Loki in disguise.
questions.why: The named holdout is the answer; the twist is that 'she' is Loki again.
questions.more: Hermod had ridden Sleipnir to Hel to negotiate the ransom.

questions.cat: Mythology · villain who was once a defender
questions.dimension: villain who was once a defender
questions.q: We remember one Egyptian god as the murderer of Osiris and lord of chaos, but in older myth he stood at the prow of the sun god's boat each night, spearing the serpent that tried to stop the sun. Which god?
questions.detail: Before his demonization, Set was the strongest god aboard Ra's solar barque, defending it against Apophis.
questions.why: The reflex is 'villain'; the slicer about defending Ra forces Set's original protective role.
questions.more: His reputation soured after the Hyksos period.

questions.cat: Mythology · ruler vs embalmer of the dead
questions.dimension: ruler vs embalmer of the dead
questions.q: In Egyptian myth the jackal-headed god handles mummies and guides souls, but he is NOT the ruler of the afterlife. Which green-skinned god actually rules the realm of the dead and judges them?
questions.detail: Anubis presides over embalming; Osiris, green-skinned for rebirth, rules and judges the afterlife.
questions.why: The shorthand makes Anubis 'the death god'; the slicer separates embalmer from ruler.
questions.more: His green skin signals resurrection and new growth.

questions.q: At the weighing of the heart, one god runs the scales and a monster eats the failures, but a different ibis-headed god stands by to write down the verdict. Who is the record-keeper?
questions.detail: Anubis operates the scales and Ammit devours the unworthy, but Thoth records the judgment.
questions.why: The scribe role uniquely picks Thoth and blocks the visceral Anubis/Ammit answers.
questions.more: Thoth is also the moon god, credited with inventing writing.

questions.cat: Mythology · trick that beat the sun god
questions.dimension: trick that beat the sun god
questions.q: An Egyptian deity made a snake from Ra's own spit, let it bite him, then offered a cure only if Ra revealed his secret name — gaining power over him. Which deity pulled off this trick?
questions.detail: Isis crafted a serpent from Ra's drool; since it came from his essence he couldn't heal himself, so he surrendered his secret name.
questions.why: The cunning here belongs to the magician Isis, not the usual trickster Set.
questions.more: Knowing a being's true name granted power over it.

questions.q: Ra sent a fierce lioness goddess to punish humans, but she went on such a killing spree that the gods flooded the fields with beer dyed red like blood to get her drunk and stop her. Which goddess nearly wiped out humanity?
questions.detail: Sekhmet, the lioness war goddess, slaughtered humans until tricked into drinking red-dyed beer.
questions.why: The lioness + beer-trick combination uniquely names Sekhmet.
questions.more: Some myths treat Sekhmet (rage) and Hathor (joy) as two faces of one goddess.

questions.q: Many myths tell of someone going to the land of the dead to retrieve a loved one and failing. In the JAPANESE version, a god goes to Yomi for his dead wife, breaks a rule by looking at her rotting body, and flees as she chases him. Which god?
questions.detail: Izanagi descended to Yomi to recover Izanami, lit a torch, saw her decaying corpse, and fled; they divorced at the underworld's border.
questions.why: The famous failed rescue is Orpheus; fixing the mythology as Japanese routes to Izanagi.
questions.more: The myth explains why more are born each day than die.

questions.q: In the AZTEC creation, a feathered-serpent god descends to the land of the dead to gather the bones of past humans, then sounds a hole-less conch to win them. Which god rebuilt humanity from bones?
questions.detail: Quetzalcoatl went to Mictlan, tricked the death lords' conch challenge with worms and bees, and anointed the bones with his own blood to make new humans.
questions.why: Routing to Aztec myth and the bone-and-conch detail pins the feathered serpent.
questions.more: His twin Xolotl, the dog-headed god, helped gather the bones.

questions.q: In a MESOPOTAMIAN myth, a goddess of love and war descends to her sister's underworld; at each of seven gates she must give up one piece of clothing, arriving naked and powerless to be struck dead. Which goddess made this descent?
questions.detail: Inanna (Akkadian Ishtar) surrendered crown, jewelry, and robes at seven gates until she stood naked before Ereshkigal.
questions.why: The seven-gate stripping uniquely identifies Inanna among descent myths.
questions.more: Her consort Dumuzi had to take her place below.

questions.cat: Mythology · first to die rules the dead
questions.dimension: first to die rules the dead
questions.q: In the HINDU Vedas, the lord of the dead earned his throne in an unusual way: he was the very first mortal ever to die, and so became the one who rules over all who follow. Which god?
questions.detail: Yama was the first mortal to die, blazing the trail to the afterlife, and thereby became its ruler and judge.
questions.why: 'Death-lord because he died first' uniquely picks Yama.
questions.more: His twin sister Yami's grief is said to have prompted the gods to create night.

questions.q: A demon won a boon: he couldn't be killed by man or beast, indoors or out, by day or night, on ground or in air. Vishnu took a form that slipped every clause at once. Which avatar killed him?
questions.detail: As Narasimha (man-lion), at twilight, on a threshold, with the demon on his lap, using claws, Vishnu killed Hiranyakashipu.
questions.why: The stacked-loophole structure pins the man-lion avatar.
questions.more: Narasimha appears to protect the devotee Prahlada.

questions.cat: Mythology · how a god got an animal head
questions.dimension: how a god got an animal head
questions.q: One Hindu god guards his mother's door, is beheaded by his own father for blocking him, and is then revived with the head of the first creature found — an elephant. Which god got his head this way?
questions.detail: Shiva beheaded Ganesha for blocking his way, then restored him with an elephant's head after Parvati's grief.
questions.why: The answer is fixed by the cause — patricidal accident and an elephant replacement — not merely 'the elephant god.'
questions.more: Some traditions say the replacement was an attendant, depicted as an elephant.

questions.cat: Mythology · the sun hides in a cave
questions.dimension: the sun hides in a cave
questions.q: In JAPANESE myth, the sun deity is offended by her storm-god brother and hides in a cave, plunging the world into darkness; the gods lure her out with a raucous dance and trick her with a mirror. Which sun deity hid?
questions.detail: Amaterasu sealed herself in Ama-no-Iwato after Susanoo's outrages; a comic dance drew her out, and a mirror transfixed her.
questions.why: Routing to Japanese myth and a female sun defeats the male-sun reflex (Ra, Helios).
questions.more: The mirror became one of Japan's three imperial regalia.

questions.q: Long before Noah, a MESOPOTAMIAN man survived a great flood in a boat, released birds to find land, and was then granted eternal life — later visited by Gilgamesh seeking that same gift. Who was this flood survivor?
questions.detail: In the Epic of Gilgamesh, Utnapishtim survived the flood, sent out birds, and was granted immortality.
questions.why: The flood-hero reflex is Noah; specifying Mesopotamia, immortality, and Gilgamesh pins Utnapishtim.
questions.more: An older version names the hero Atrahasis.

questions.q: An Irish god talked his way into the gods' hall not by one great skill but by claiming mastery of every art at once — and when no single god there could match all of them, the doors opened. Which god earned the title 'Master of All Arts'?
questions.detail: Lugh gained entry to the Tuatha De Danann as Samildanach, 'master of all arts.'
questions.why: The defining trait is being a polymath rather than a specialist, which uniquely names Lugh.
questions.more: Lugh later killed the one-eyed Balor.

questions.q: Egypt has its jackal-headed guide of the dead, but the AZTECs had a dog-headed god — twin of the feathered serpent — who guided souls through the underworld. Which god?
questions.detail: Xolotl, the dog-headed twin of Quetzalcoatl, guided souls and helped fetch the bones of humanity.
questions.why: The dog-guide reflex is Anubis; fixing it as Aztec and 'twin of the feathered serpent' routes to Xolotl.
questions.more: The Xoloitzcuintli dog takes its name from him.

questions.q: After Baldr died, one of Odin's sons borrowed the eight-legged horse Sleipnir and rode nine nights down to Hel to try to ransom him. Which god made the ride?
questions.detail: Hermod rode Sleipnir to Hel and secured Hel's terms — that all things weep for Baldr.
questions.why: The rider to Hel is the obscure-by-reflex but reasoning-reachable Hermod, distinct from the famous gods.
questions.more: The bargain failed when Thokk refused to weep.

questions.q: One Norse god was said to be born of nine mothers, need less sleep than a bird, and hear wool growing on a sheep's back. He guards the rainbow bridge into Asgard. Who is he?
questions.detail: Heimdall, watchman of the gods, guards Bifrost, has nine mothers, and will sound the Gjallarhorn at Ragnarok.
questions.why: The nine-mothers birth plus hyper-acute senses uniquely identify Heimdall.
questions.more: He and Loki are fated to kill each other.
